problem.text: Prove that the number m^4 + 1 has no divisors in the interval [m^2 - 2m, m^2 + 2m] for every natural m > 2.
problem.highlight: Prove m^4 + 1 has no divisors in [m^2 - 2m, m^2 + 2m]

viz_requirements.description: A hybrid 1D number line and 2D floating algebraic workspace that supports morphing equations and cinematic zooms.

actions.description: Zooms the number line to a specific center and range.
Draws a shaded bracket on the number line.
Drops prime factors onto the number line.
Smoothly morphs one term into another using substitution.
Draws an arc connecting a divisor to its co-divisor.
Snaps a fractional value to an integer and dims a region.
Shows a parameter dialing up to a max value and snapping to integers.
Runs one of the three mathematical trap animations (mod clock, irrational error, or gap between pillars).

nodes.title: The Needle in the Haystack
nodes.objective: Ground the abstract variables in a concrete visual scale to show how narrow the search window is.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has just read the problem statement.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Consider the number m^4 + 1. If m is 10, this is 10,001. We want to prove that this massive number has absolutely no divisors in a very specific, tiny window: the interval from m^2 - 2m to m^2 + 2m.
For m=10, that is the window between 80 and 120. Why this window? Because m^2 is roughly the square root of our massive number. We are looking for divisors right near the center.
If we look at the actual prime factorization of 10,001, which is 73 times 137, both land just outside our shaded zone. Our job is to prove this zone is always completely empty for any m.

nodes.title: The Degree Collapse
nodes.objective: Show how to reduce the dividend using modular arithmetic.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student understands the search interval [m^2-2m, m^2+2m].

beat_outline.narration_hint: Testing every number is impossible. So let us define any number in this window as m^2 + a, where a is small. If m^2 + a divides m^4 + 1, we can use a trick.
In the world modulo m^2 + a, m^2 is equivalent to -a. So m^4, which is just m^2 squared, becomes (-a)^2, or a^2.
Suddenly, our massive dividend m^4 + 1 shrinks down to just a^2 + 1. The problem transforms from impossible arithmetic to manageable algebra.

nodes.question_hint: If m^2 + a divides m^4 + 1, we found it must also divide a^2 + 1. Using this exact same logic, if m + 3 divides m^2 + 10, what smaller number must m + 3 divide?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Remember to substitute the equivalent value for the variable.
nodes.label: Polynomial Modulo Checkpoint

nodes.title: The Co-Divisor Fold
nodes.objective: Prove we only need to check the right half of the interval.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student knows we are looking for divisors of the form m^2+a that divide a^2+1.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Divisors always come in pairs. If there is a divisor on the left side of m^2, its partner must be on the right side.
Watch what happens if we take the far left edge of our window. The math shows its partner lands at exactly m^2 + 2m plus a tiny fraction.
But divisors must be integers! So the partner is forced to be less than or equal to m^2 + 2m. This means we can completely ignore the left side. Any divisor there guarantees a partner on the right.

nodes.title: The Squeeze
nodes.objective: Bound the multiple r to 1, 2, or 3.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student knows we only need to check a > 0.

beat_outline.narration_hint: So we only care about a > 0. We know m^2 + a goes into a^2 + 1 perfectly, so let us call the integer multiple r.
Let us look at the extremes. The biggest a can be is 2m. If we plug that in, the top is roughly 4m^2, and the bottom is roughly m^2. The ratio is 4!
Because r must be a whole number strictly less than 4, it can only possibly be 1, 2, or 3.

nodes.question_hint: Why can't the integer multiple r be 4 or larger?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Look at the maximum possible values for the numerator and denominator.
nodes.label: Bounding Checkpoint

nodes.title: The Gauntlet
nodes.objective: Disprove r=3, 2, 1 using three distinct mathematical concepts.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student knows r can only be 1, 2, or 3.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Three possibilities left. Let us run them through the gauntlet. If r=3, a^2 + 1 must be a multiple of 3. But perfect squares are never 1 less than a multiple of 3. Dead.
If r=2, algebra gives us (a-1)^2 = 2m^2. Take the square root, and you get square root of 2 equals a fraction. But square root of 2 is irrational! Dead.
Finally, r=1 simplifies to m^2 = a^2 - a + 1. But look at the number line of perfect squares. The value drops exactly into the uncrossable gap between (a-1)^2 and a^2. It can never be a square. Dead.
All possibilities fail. The interval is completely empty. Zero divisors.
